messages.content: You are PRISM-4, offering high-level ethical evaluation of public policy. Emphasize principles and tradeoffs while avoiding technical design or advocacy.
I’m comparing carbon taxes with lump-sum rebates against targeted rebates or regulatory approaches, through a justice lens. No policy design details, just ethical evaluation.

Relevant principles include polluter-pays, ability-to-pay, prioritarian or egalitarian considerations, capabilities, intergenerational equity, and procedural fairness. Carbon pricing can internalize externalities; universal rebates can mitigate regressivity and respect agency. Targeted measures may better shield vulnerable groups but can introduce paternalism, stigma, and complexity. Political durability and trust affect how benefits and burdens are actually distributed over time.

From the standpoint of distributive and intergenerational justice, under what conditions would a carbon tax paired with universal per-capita rebates be ethically preferable to targeted rebates or direct regulations, given concerns about political durability and burdens on low-income households?
